explicit_text: I was drafted into the United States Army in January 1951 during the Korean War. I went through basic training at Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri, which was pretty easy because they needed warm bodies quickly for Korea. I served as a motor sergeant in the 3rd Medical Battalion, part of the 3rd Infantry Division, and took pride in my role maintaining vehicles and generators in harsh conditions. The Army gave me a sense of purpose and responsibility, even though I was initially a reluctant draftee.

It wasn’t much of a shock to me, and I felt it was somewhat of a waste of time since they pushed us through fast. Still, it was my first formal military experience before heading overseas.

explicit_text: I was drafted into the Army in 1951 right in the middle of the Korean War. The fighting was intense, with the enemy pushing us around, so the Army needed replacements fast. I was sent over as an individual replacement to Inchon, Korea, and served in the 3rd Infantry Division’s medical battalion. The winters were brutally cold, sometimes 25 below zero, and we had to move camps frequently due to guerrilla attacks. Despite the hardships, we were well supplied and supported throughout the war.

explicit_text: I landed at Inchon, Korea, after traveling through Japan for a few days. Inchon was a huge replacement depot with about 25,000 GIs, where soldiers like me were assigned to units as needed. From there, I was sent to the front lines with the 3rd Infantry Division. Inchon was an important staging area during the Korean War, and it was where I first set foot in the combat zone.

That port was an important staging area during the Korean conflict, and it was where I first set foot in the combat zone.

explicit_text: After arriving in Korea, I was assigned to the 3rd Infantry Division, specifically the 3rd Medical Battalion. This division was right at the front lines, and we had to move camps every month or two because of guerrilla activity. I served as a motor sergeant in the medical battalion, which was a small unit with about thirty personnel and one doctor. The 3rd Infantry Division was where I really found my place in the Army during the Korean War.

explicit_text: I was part of the 3rd Medical Battalion’s Clearing Company in Korea. It was a small outfit, with just one doctor and around thirty personnel, much like the TV show M*A*S*H, though less colorful. We were mostly young draftees who didn’t know much about the Army or Korea when we arrived. My role was as a mechanic and motor sergeant, taking care of all the vehicles and electrical equipment, which made me feel indispensable.

explicit_text: People often compare my unit, the 3rd Medical Battalion, to the TV show M*A*S*H. We had a small medical outfit with one doctor and about thirty personnel, similar to what you see on the show. However, unlike the colorful characters on TV, we were mostly young, inexperienced draftees who didn’t know much about the Army or Korea. Still, the comparison helps people understand the kind of medical support unit we were during the Korean War.

explicit_text: I heard about the new book by Halberstam called The Coldest Winter, which covers the Korean War. It reminded me of how brutally cold the winters were when I served, with temperatures sometimes 25 below zero and dampness that got into your bones. The book’s title really captures the harsh conditions we faced in the mountains during the war. It’s a good reminder of the challenges we endured back then.

explicit_text: After leaving the United States, I traveled by boat to Japan and stayed there for about five or six days before heading to Korea. Japan served as a transit point for many of us en route to the Korean War front. We also received supplies from Japan, like fresh vegetables and ice cream, which were flown in by helicopter to our camps in Korea. Japan was an important logistical hub during the war.

That country served as a transit point for many of us en route to the Korean War front. We also received supplies from there, like fresh vegetables and ice cream, which were flown in by helicopter to our camps in Korea. It was an important logistical hub during the war.

explicit_text: Our unit had two South Korean soldiers attached to us, who were called ROCs. They were part of the Republic of Korea Army and worked alongside us in the 3rd Medical Battalion. Interestingly, these Korean soldiers preferred to eat native food rather than the American rations we had. Their presence was a reminder of the cooperation between American and South Korean forces during the war.

explicit_text: I heard some veterans talk about the Imjin River, but I don’t recall it personally. However, I do remember swimming in a strong river near our camp, which had a very powerful current that almost swept me away. We didn’t know the names of many rivers where we were stationed, but the currents were definitely dangerous. The Imjin River is well known among Korean War vets, though it didn’t ring a bell for me at the time.

explicit_text: While stationed at Kowang, our unit came under artillery fire a couple of times, with shells landing a few hundred yards away. We didn’t know where the shells came from, but it was a reminder that we were in a combat zone. After that, we were instructed to build foxholes covered with logs for protection, although they were often unsafe and prone to collapsing. Kowang was one of the places where I experienced the dangers of the front lines during the Korean War.
